title: Thalassaemia
oneLiner: Inherited haemoglobin synthesis disorder causing microcytic anaemia; ranges from asymptomatic trait to transfusion-dependent major requiring lifelong chelation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute haemolytic crisis (rare, usually triggered by infection or oxidant drugs):** sudden pallor, dark urine, jaundice, splenic pain → if Hb drops rapidly with haemolysis markers (high LDH, low haptoglobin) → emergency transfusion + haematology review.
- **Cardiac failure from severe anaemia (Hb <50 g/L) or iron overload cardiomyopathy:** look for raised JVP, gallop rhythm, pulmonary oedema. Single bedside clue: **tachycardia disproportionate to fever** or **hypotension with high-output failure**.
- **Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) ** in any asplenic thalassaemia patient (post-splenectomy or functional hyposplenism): **sudden rigors, high fever, hypotension** → treat as sepsis with IV antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone 2g IV) immediately, check if they have had pneumococcal/meningococcal/Hib vaccination.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
	- Fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea on exertion (anaemia)
	- Jaundice (haemolysis)
	- Abdominal fullness/pain (splenomegaly)
	- Bone pain, frontal bossing, maxillary hypertrophy (if severe, especially β-thalassaemia major)
	- Growth delay / failure to thrive (children)
- **Key exam findings:**
	- Pallor, icterus
	- Splenomegaly (common in thalassaemia intermedia/major; may be massive)
	- Hepatomegaly
	- Frontal bossing, maxillary prominence, 'chipmunk facies' (severe disease)
	- Leg ulcers (chronic haemolysis)
	- Cardiac signs: flow murmur, signs of high-output failure if severe anaemia
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Iron deficiency anaemia (IDA):** most common mimic; check ferritin <12-15 µg/L, transferrin saturation <16%, low MCV, high RDW
- **Anaemia of chronic disease:** mild microcytosis, ferritin normal/high, transferrin saturation low/normal
- **Sideroblastic anaemia:** dimorphic blood film, ring sideroblasts on bone marrow
- **HbE disease:** common in SE Asia; Hb electrophoresis key
- **Other haemoglobinopathies** (e.g. sickle cell disease with microcytosis if α-thalassaemia co-inherited)
- **Splitter:** **Normal or high ferritin + microcytic anaemia → think thalassaemia (or ACD) not IDA.** RDW normal in thalassaemia trait, high in IDA.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
	- Record NEWS2, urine dip for blood (haemoglobinuria may indicate acute haemolysis)
	- Check for splenomegaly (US if unsure)
- **Bloods:**
	- FBC, blood film (target cells, basophilic stippling in thalassaemia)
	- Hb electrophoresis / HPLC (diagnostic)
	- Ferritin, transferrin saturation, iron studies (to exclude IDA)
	- LDH, haptoglobin (haemolysis markers)
	- Reticulocyte count
	- Blood group and antibody screen (if transfusion anticipated)
	- If suspected acute haemolytic crisis: direct Coombs test, bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin, urine haemosiderin
	- Check Hb electrophoresis in **all** microcytic anaemia with normal ferritin
- **Imaging:**
	- Chest X-ray (if breathless/cardiorespiratory symptoms)
	- Abdominal ultrasound (spleen size, gallstones)
- **Special tests:**
	- Genetic testing (α- or β-globin gene mutations) for family screening or uncertain cases
- **Never-miss test:** **Hb electrophoresis/HPLC in any microcytic anaemia with normal or high ferritin** — omission leads to misdiagnosis as IDA and unnecessary iron therapy (which is dangerous in thalassaemia major/intermedia).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
	- Severe anaemia (Hb <70 g/L) with haemodynamic compromise: crossmatch and transfuse **packed red cells** (single unit, then reassess). If heart failure, give furosemide 20-40mg IV post-transfusion.
	- Suspected OPSI (fever, rigors, asplenic): give IV ceftriaxone 2g stat + fluid resuscitation, escalate to sepsis pathway.
	- Acute haemolysis: contact haematology; may require exchange transfusion if severe.
	- Obtain Hb electrophoresis and ferritin before transfusion if possible (to avoid masking diagnosis).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
	- If transfusion-dependent: maintain Hb >90-100 g/L (as per local policy). Check ferritin and iron overload status.
	- If non-transfusion dependent (thalassaemia intermedia/trait): manage anaemia symptomatically, monitor for complications.
	- Folic acid 5mg daily (supports erythropoiesis).
	- Assess spleen size; if massive and causing mechanical symptoms or thrombocytopenia → discuss splenectomy (with pre-op vaccinations).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
	- Transfusion-dependent (β-thalassaemia major): regular transfusions (every 2-5 weeks) + iron chelation therapy (desferrioxamine SC/IV or oral deferasirox/deferiprone — follow haematology guidance).
	- Non-transfusion dependent: monitor Hb, manage complications (gallstones, leg ulcers, pulmonary hypertension, thrombosis).
	- Hypogonadism, hypothyroidism, diabetes (iron overload): screen annually.
	- Bone density screening (osteoporosis risk, especially if hypogonadal).
	- Vaccination as per asplenia/hyposplenia guidelines (pneumococcal, meningococcal ACWY+B, Hib, influenza).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
	- **Fluids:** IV access but no specific fluid regimen unless transfusion-related; maintain hydration.
	- **VTE prophylaxis:** consider if Hb low (risk of thrombosis in thalassaemia intermedia). Use prophylactic LMWH unless contraindicated (e.g. bleeding risk) — check weight and renal function.
	- **Diet:** normal.
	- **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable).
	- **Medication:** folic acid 5mg PO daily; continue chelation as per haematology plan.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
	- **Folic acid:** 5mg PO once daily (all thalassaemia patients with ongoing haemolysis).
	- **Iron chelators** (for iron overload): **Desferrioxamine** (SC infusion 20-40 mg/kg/day over 8-12h, or IV for acute iron toxicity). **Deferasirox** (oral tablet, 20-30 mg/kg once daily). **Deferiprone** (oral, 75-100 mg/kg/day in three divided doses). *Doses must be individualised — refer to local haematology protocols.*
	- **Transfusion:** packed red cells, usually 10-15 mL/kg over 3-4h.
- **Common alternatives:**
	- Combined deferiprone + deferasirox for difficult-to-control iron overload.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
	- Desferrioxamine: avoid in renal failure without dose adjustment.
	- Deferasirox: contraindicated in CrCl <30 mL/min (avoid in advanced renal impairment).
	- Deferiprone: risk of agranulocytosis (check weekly FBC).
	- Folic acid: safe.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
	- Deferasirox: reduce dose if CrCl 30-60 mL/min.
	- Desferrioxamine: adjust based on iron levels; no major renal dose change but monitor.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
	- **Starting iron chelation without confirming iron overload** — can cause serious side effects (desferrioxamine toxicity: ototoxicity, growth retardation; deferasirox: renal failure). Only initiate after demonstrated iron overload (ferritin >1000 µg/L, or >500 with cardiac/liver iron loading). Also, **prescribing oral iron supplements in thalassaemia trait or major** — can worsen iron overload and is ineffective.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
	- Severe anaemia (cardiorespiratory failure)
	- Acute haemolytic crisis (induced by infection, oxidant drugs)
	- Splenic sequestration (pain, sudden drop in Hb, thrombocytopenia)
	- OPSI (asplenic)
	- Thromboembolism (especially thalassaemia intermedia)
	- Gallstone cholecystitis
- **Chronic:**
	- Iron overload: cardiomyopathy, liver fibrosis, endocrinopathies (diabetes, hypogonadism, hypothyroidism)
	- Hypersplenism (cytopenias, mechanical symptoms)
	- Skeletal deformities (if undertransfused)
	- Osteoporosis (extra risk from hypogonadism and iron chelators)
	- Leg ulcers (chronic haemolysis)
	- Pulmonary hypertension (thalassaemia intermedia)
	- Increased risk of infection (asplenia, iron overload)
	- Growth delay and delayed puberty in children
sections.Prognosis:
- **Thalassaemia major:** if well-transfused and chelated, survival into 5th-6th decade. Without chelation, death from iron overload in teens/20s.
- **Thalassaemia intermedia:** variable, often moderate anaemia with complications; risk of thromboembolism and pulmonary hypertension.
- **Thalassaemia trait:** normal life expectancy, no treatment needed; mild microcytic anaemia, rarely requires intervention.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check ferritin and transferrin saturation in every microcytic anaemia** before starting iron — prevents misdiagnosis and iatrogenic iron overload.
- **If thalassaemia is suspected, request Hb electrophoresis/HPLC early** (it’s the gold standard). Don’t wait for haematinics.
- **In known thalassaemia major, always document iron overload status** (ferritin, cardiac T2* MRI) and ensure chelation is prescribed and adhered to.
- **In asplenic patients (post-splenectomy or hyposplenism), give emergency antibiotics at first sign of infection** (even before culture) — can be life-saving.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never prescribe oral iron for anaemia without confirming iron deficiency** in a patient with known thalassaemia or microcytosis — this is a major cause of preventable iron overload.
- **Do not delay transfusion in severe anaemia (Hb <50 g/L) with heart failure** — even if the aetiology is unclear, give packed cells slowly with diuretic cover.
- **Do not discharge an asplenic patient with fever without IV antibiotics and a clear safety net** — OPSI can kill within hours.
- **Do not forget folic acid** — ongoing haemolysis can cause folate deficiency and worsen anaemia.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
	- Fever >38°C (especially if asplenic)
	- Shortness of breath, palpitations, chest pain
	- Sudden pallor, jaundice, dark urine (haemolytic crisis)
	- Severe abdominal pain (splenic sequestration or cholecystitis)
- **Follow-up:**
	- Specialist haematology clinic (regular for transfusion/chelation)
	- Routine checks: ferritin, FBC, cardiac and endocrine surveillance
	- Vaccination schedule for asplenia (repeat pneumococcal every 5 years)
	- Genetic counselling for relatives (if newly diagnosed)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or clinical concern
- **Acute severe anaemia (Hb <60 g/L) with cardiorespiratory compromise** → urgent haematology review + transfusion
- **Suspected OPSI** high risk NEWS2, fever with rigors, hypotension → sepsis pathway, IV antibiotics, senior review immediately
- **Suspected acute haemolytic crisis** → haematology on-call & consider exchange transfusion
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L, pH <7.3, or GCS <13** → ICU outreach / medical emergency team (MET)
- **When in doubt about iron chelation doses or transfusion thresholds** → ask haematology senior before prescribing

rawMarkdown:
## Thalassaemia
**One-liner:** Inherited haemoglobin synthesis disorder causing microcytic anaemia; ranges from asymptomatic trait to transfusion-dependent major requiring lifelong chelation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute haemolytic crisis (rare, usually triggered by infection or oxidant drugs):** sudden pallor, dark urine, jaundice, splenic pain → if Hb drops rapidly with haemolysis markers (high LDH, low haptoglobin) → emergency transfusion + haematology review.
- **Cardiac failure from severe anaemia (Hb <50 g/L) or iron overload cardiomyopathy:** look for raised JVP, gallop rhythm, pulmonary oedema. Single bedside clue: **tachycardia disproportionate to fever** or **hypotension with high-output failure**.
- **Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) ** in any asplenic thalassaemia patient (post-splenectomy or functional hyposplenism): **sudden rigors, high fever, hypotension** → treat as sepsis with IV antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone 2g IV) immediately, check if they have had pneumococcal/meningococcal/Hib vaccination.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
	- Fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea on exertion (anaemia)
	- Jaundice (haemolysis)
	- Abdominal fullness/pain (splenomegaly)
	- Bone pain, frontal bossing, maxillary hypertrophy (if severe, especially β-thalassaemia major)
	- Growth delay / failure to thrive (children)
- **Key exam findings:**
	- Pallor, icterus
	- Splenomegaly (common in thalassaemia intermedia/major; may be massive)
	- Hepatomegaly
	- Frontal bossing, maxillary prominence, 'chipmunk facies' (severe disease)
	- Leg ulcers (chronic haemolysis)
	- Cardiac signs: flow murmur, signs of high-output failure if severe anaemia

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Iron deficiency anaemia (IDA):** most common mimic; check ferritin <12-15 µg/L, transferrin saturation <16%, low MCV, high RDW
- **Anaemia of chronic disease:** mild microcytosis, ferritin normal/high, transferrin saturation low/normal
- **Sideroblastic anaemia:** dimorphic blood film, ring sideroblasts on bone marrow
- **HbE disease:** common in SE Asia; Hb electrophoresis key
- **Other haemoglobinopathies** (e.g. sickle cell disease with microcytosis if α-thalassaemia co-inherited)
- **Splitter:** **Normal or high ferritin + microcytic anaemia → think thalassaemia (or ACD) not IDA.** RDW normal in thalassaemia trait, high in IDA.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
	- Record NEWS2, urine dip for blood (haemoglobinuria may indicate acute haemolysis)
	- Check for splenomegaly (US if unsure)
- **Bloods:**
	- FBC, blood film (target cells, basophilic stippling in thalassaemia)
	- Hb electrophoresis / HPLC (diagnostic)
	- Ferritin, transferrin saturation, iron studies (to exclude IDA)
	- LDH, haptoglobin (haemolysis markers)
	- Reticulocyte count
	- Blood group and antibody screen (if transfusion anticipated)
	- If suspected acute haemolytic crisis: direct Coombs test, bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin, urine haemosiderin
	- Check Hb electrophoresis in **all** microcytic anaemia with normal ferritin
- **Imaging:**
	- Chest X-ray (if breathless/cardiorespiratory symptoms)
	- Abdominal ultrasound (spleen size, gallstones)
- **Special tests:**
	- Genetic testing (α- or β-globin gene mutations) for family screening or uncertain cases
- **Never-miss test:** **Hb electrophoresis/HPLC in any microcytic anaemia with normal or high ferritin** — omission leads to misdiagnosis as IDA and unnecessary iron therapy (which is dangerous in thalassaemia major/intermedia).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
	- Severe anaemia (Hb <70 g/L) with haemodynamic compromise: crossmatch and transfuse **packed red cells** (single unit, then reassess). If heart failure, give furosemide 20-40mg IV post-transfusion.
	- Suspected OPSI (fever, rigors, asplenic): give IV ceftriaxone 2g stat + fluid resuscitation, escalate to sepsis pathway.
	- Acute haemolysis: contact haematology; may require exchange transfusion if severe.
	- Obtain Hb electrophoresis and ferritin before transfusion if possible (to avoid masking diagnosis).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
	- If transfusion-dependent: maintain Hb >90-100 g/L (as per local policy). Check ferritin and iron overload status.
	- If non-transfusion dependent (thalassaemia intermedia/trait): manage anaemia symptomatically, monitor for complications.
	- Folic acid 5mg daily (supports erythropoiesis).
	- Assess spleen size; if massive and causing mechanical symptoms or thrombocytopenia → discuss splenectomy (with pre-op vaccinations).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
	- Transfusion-dependent (β-thalassaemia major): regular transfusions (every 2-5 weeks) + iron chelation therapy (desferrioxamine SC/IV or oral deferasirox/deferiprone — follow haematology guidance).
	- Non-transfusion dependent: monitor Hb, manage complications (gallstones, leg ulcers, pulmonary hypertension, thrombosis).
	- Hypogonadism, hypothyroidism, diabetes (iron overload): screen annually.
	- Bone density screening (osteoporosis risk, especially if hypogonadal).
	- Vaccination as per asplenia/hyposplenia guidelines (pneumococcal, meningococcal ACWY+B, Hib, influenza).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
	- **Fluids:** IV access but no specific fluid regimen unless transfusion-related; maintain hydration.
	- **VTE prophylaxis:** consider if Hb low (risk of thrombosis in thalassaemia intermedia). Use prophylactic LMWH unless contraindicated (e.g. bleeding risk) — check weight and renal function.
	- **Diet:** normal.
	- **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable).
	- **Medication:** folic acid 5mg PO daily; continue chelation as per haematology plan.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
	- **Folic acid:** 5mg PO once daily (all thalassaemia patients with ongoing haemolysis).
	- **Iron chelators** (for iron overload): **Desferrioxamine** (SC infusion 20-40 mg/kg/day over 8-12h, or IV for acute iron toxicity). **Deferasirox** (oral tablet, 20-30 mg/kg once daily). **Deferiprone** (oral, 75-100 mg/kg/day in three divided doses). *Doses must be individualised — refer to local haematology protocols.*
	- **Transfusion:** packed red cells, usually 10-15 mL/kg over 3-4h.
- **Common alternatives:**
	- Combined deferiprone + deferasirox for difficult-to-control iron overload.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
	- Desferrioxamine: avoid in renal failure without dose adjustment.
	- Deferasirox: contraindicated in CrCl <30 mL/min (avoid in advanced renal impairment).
	- Deferiprone: risk of agranulocytosis (check weekly FBC).
	- Folic acid: safe.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
	- Deferasirox: reduce dose if CrCl 30-60 mL/min.
	- Desferrioxamine: adjust based on iron levels; no major renal dose change but monitor.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
	- **Starting iron chelation without confirming iron overload** — can cause serious side effects (desferrioxamine toxicity: ototoxicity, growth retardation; deferasirox: renal failure). Only initiate after demonstrated iron overload (ferritin >1000 µg/L, or >500 with cardiac/liver iron loading). Also, **prescribing oral iron supplements in thalassaemia trait or major** — can worsen iron overload and is ineffective.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
	- Severe anaemia (cardiorespiratory failure)
	- Acute haemolytic crisis (induced by infection, oxidant drugs)
	- Splenic sequestration (pain, sudden drop in Hb, thrombocytopenia)
	- OPSI (asplenic)
	- Thromboembolism (especially thalassaemia intermedia)
	- Gallstone cholecystitis
- **Chronic:**
	- Iron overload: cardiomyopathy, liver fibrosis, endocrinopathies (diabetes, hypogonadism, hypothyroidism)
	- Hypersplenism (cytopenias, mechanical symptoms)
	- Skeletal deformities (if undertransfused)
	- Osteoporosis (extra risk from hypogonadism and iron chelators)
	- Leg ulcers (chronic haemolysis)
	- Pulmonary hypertension (thalassaemia intermedia)
	- Increased risk of infection (asplenia, iron overload)
	- Growth delay and delayed puberty in children

### Prognosis
- **Thalassaemia major:** if well-transfused and chelated, survival into 5th-6th decade. Without chelation, death from iron overload in teens/20s.
- **Thalassaemia intermedia:** variable, often moderate anaemia with complications; risk of thromboembolism and pulmonary hypertension.
- **Thalassaemia trait:** normal life expectancy, no treatment needed; mild microcytic anaemia, rarely requires intervention.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check ferritin and transferrin saturation in every microcytic anaemia** before starting iron — prevents misdiagnosis and iatrogenic iron overload.
- **If thalassaemia is suspected, request Hb electrophoresis/HPLC early** (it’s the gold standard). Don’t wait for haematinics.
- **In known thalassaemia major, always document iron overload status** (ferritin, cardiac T2* MRI) and ensure chelation is prescribed and adhered to.
- **In asplenic patients (post-splenectomy or hyposplenism), give emergency antibiotics at first sign of infection** (even before culture) — can be life-saving.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never prescribe oral iron for anaemia without confirming iron deficiency** in a patient with known thalassaemia or microcytosis — this is a major cause of preventable iron overload.
- **Do not delay transfusion in severe anaemia (Hb <50 g/L) with heart failure** — even if the aetiology is unclear, give packed cells slowly with diuretic cover.
- **Do not discharge an asplenic patient with fever without IV antibiotics and a clear safety net** — OPSI can kill within hours.
- **Do not forget folic acid** — ongoing haemolysis can cause folate deficiency and worsen anaemia.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
	- Fever >38°C (especially if asplenic)
	- Shortness of breath, palpitations, chest pain
	- Sudden pallor, jaundice, dark urine (haemolytic crisis)
	- Severe abdominal pain (splenic sequestration or cholecystitis)
- **Follow-up:**
	- Specialist haematology clinic (regular for transfusion/chelation)
	- Routine checks: ferritin, FBC, cardiac and endocrine surveillance
	- Vaccination schedule for asplenia (repeat pneumococcal every 5 years)
	- Genetic counselling for relatives (if newly diagnosed)

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or clinical concern
- **Acute severe anaemia (Hb <60 g/L) with cardiorespiratory compromise** → urgent haematology review + transfusion
- **Suspected OPSI** high risk NEWS2, fever with rigors, hypotension → sepsis pathway, IV antibiotics, senior review immediately
- **Suspected acute haemolytic crisis** → haematology on-call & consider exchange transfusion
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L, pH <7.3, or GCS <13** → ICU outreach / medical emergency team (MET)
- **When in doubt about iron chelation doses or transfusion thresholds** → ask haematology senior before prescribing
